la: in music, the syllable by which Guido denotes the last sound of each hexachord.
lab: a great talker; a blabber. Obs.
labadist: the Labadists were followers of Jean de Labadie, who lived in the 17th century. They held that God can and does deceive men, that the observance of the sabbath is a matter of indifference, and other peculiar or heretical opinions.
labefaction: A weakening or loosening; a failing; decay; downfall; ruin.
label: noun

A narrow slip of silk, paper or parchment, containing a name or title, and affixed to any thing, denoting its contents. Such are the labels affixed to the vessels of an apothecary. Labels also are affixed to deeds or writings to hold the appended seal.
Any paper annexed to a will by way of addition; as a codicil.

In heraldry, a fillet usually placed in the middle, along the chief of the coat, without touching its extremities. It is adorned with pendants, and used on the arms of the eldest son, to distinguish him from the younger sons, while the father is living.
A long thin brass rule, with a small sight at one end, and a center-hole at the other, commonly used with a tangent line on the edge of a circumferentor, to take altitudes.
verb transitive
To affix a label to.
labial: Pertaining to the lips; formed by the lips; as a labial articulation. Thus b, p, and m are labial articulations.
A letter or character representing an articulation of the lips; as b, f, m, p
labiate: a2. In botany, a labiate corol is irregular, monopetalous, with two lips, or monopetalous, consisting of a narrow tube with a wide mouth, divided into two or more segments arranged in two opposite divisions or lips. a labiate flower has a labiate corol.
labiated: a2. In botany, a labiate corol is irregular, monopetalous, with two lips, or monopetalous, consisting of a narrow tube with a wide mouth, divided into two or more segments arranged in two opposite divisions or lips. a labiate flower has a labiate corol.
labile: Liable to err, fall or apostatize.
labiodental: a2. Formed or pronounced by the cooperation of the lips and teeth; as f and v.
labor: Exertion of muscular strength, or bodily exertion which occasions weariness; particularly, the exertion of the limbs in occupations by which subsistence is obtained, as in agriculture and manufactures, in distinction from exertions of strength in play or amusements, which are denominated exercise, rather than labor. Toilsome work; pains; travail; any bodily exertion which is attended with fatigue. After the labors of the day, the farmer retires, and rest is sweet. Moderate labor contributes to health. What is obtained by labor will of right be the property of him by whose labor it is gained.
Intellectual exertion; application of the mind which occasions weariness; as the labor of compiling and writing a history.
Exertion of mental powers, united with bodily employment; as the labors of the apostles in propagating christianity.
Work done, or to be done; that which requires wearisome exertion. Being a labor of so great difficulty, the exact performance thereof we may rather wish than look for.
Heroic achievement; as the labors of Hercules.&
laboratory: A house or place where operations and experiments in chimistry, pharmacy, pyrotechny, are performed.
A place where arms are manufactured or repaired, or fire-works prepared; as the laboratory in Springfield, in Massachusetts.
A place where work is performed, or any thing is prepared for use. Hence the stomach is called the grand laboratory of the human body; the liver, the laboratory of the bile.
laborer: One who labors in a toilsome occupation; a man who does work that requires little skill, as distinguished from an artisan.
laboring: Exerting muscular strength or intellectual power; toiling; moving with pain or with difficulty; cultivating.

A laboring man, or laborer, is often used for a man who performs work that requires no apprenticeship or professional skill, in distinction from an artisan; but this restricted sense is not always observed. A hard laboring man, is one accustomed to hard labor.
laborious: Using exertion; employing labor; diligent in work or service; assiduous; used of persons; as a laborious husbandman or mechanic; a laborious minister or pastor.
Requiring labor; toilsome; tiresome; not easy; as laborious duties or services.
Requiring labor, exertion, perseverance or sacrifices. Dost thou love watchings, abstinence or toil, laborious virtues all? Learn these from Cato.
laboriousness: The quality of being laborious, or attended with toil; toilsomeness; difficulty.
laborsome: Made with great labor and diligence.
laburnum: A tree of the genus Cytisus.
labyrinth: Among the ancients, an edifice or place full of intricacies, or formed with winding passages, which rendered it difficult to find the way from the interior to the entrance. The most remarkable of these edifices mentioned, are the Egyptian and the Cretan labyrinths.
Formerly, an ornamental maze or wilderness in gardens.
lac: Gum-lac, so called, but improperly, not being a gum, but a resin. It is deposited on different species of trees in the East Indies, by an insect called Chermes lacca. Stick lac is the substance in its natural state, encrusting small twigs. When broken off and boiled in water, it loses its red color, and is called seed lac. When melted and reduced to a thin crust, it is call shell lac. United with ivory black or vermilion, it forms black and red sealing wax. A solution with borax, colored by lampblack, constitutes Indian ink. Lac dissolved in alcohol or other menstrua, by different methods of preparation, constitutes various kinds of varnishes and lackers.
laccic: Pertaining to lac, or produced from it; as laccic acid.
lace: A work composed of threads interwoven into a net, and worked on a pillow with spindles or pins. Fine laces are manufactured in France, Italy and England.
A plaited string with which females fasten their clothes. Doll ne’er was called to cut her lace.
To fasten with a string through eyelet holes. When Jenny’s stays are newly laced - .
lace-bark: A shrub in the West Indies, the Daphue lagetto, so called from the texture of its inner bark.
laced: Fastened with lace or a string; also, tricked off with lace. Laced coffee, coffee with spirits in it.
laceman: A man who deals in lace.
lacewoman: A woman who makes or sells lace.
lacerate: To tear; to rend; to separate a substance by violence or tearing; as, to lacerate the flesh. It is applied chiefly to the flesh, or figuratively to the heart. But sometimes it is applied to the political or civil divisions in a state.
In botany, having the edge variously cut into irregular segments; as a lacerated leaf.
lacerated: In botany, having the edge variously cut into irregular segments; as a lacerated leaf.
laceration: The act of tearing or rending; the breach made by rending.
lacerative: Tearing; having the power to tear; as lacerative humors.
lacertus: The girroc, a fish of the gar-fish kind; also, the lizard-fish.
lachrymal: Generating or secreting tears; as the lachrymal gland.
lachrymatory: A vessel found in sepulchers of the ancients, in which it has been supposed the tears of a deceased person’s friends were collected and preserved with the ashes and urn. It was a small glass or bottle like a phial.
lacing: Fastening with a string; adorned or trimmed with lace.
lack: To want; to be destitute of; not to have or possess. If any of you lack wisdom, let him ask it of God - James 1:5.
To be in want. The young lions do lack and suffer hunger. Psalm 34:10.
To be wanting. Perhaps there shall lack five of the fifty righteous. Genesis 18:28.

Want; destitution; need; failure. He that gathered little, had no lack. Exodus 16:18. Lack of rupees is one hundred thousand rupees, which at 55 cents each, amount to fifty five thousand dollars, or at 2s. 6d. sterling, to 12,500 pounds.
lackbrain: One that wants brains, or is deficient in understanding.
lacker: A kind of varnish. The basis of lackers is a solution of the substance called seed-lack or shell-lack, in spirit of wine or alcohol. Varnishes applied to metals improve their color and preserve them from tarnishing. Lackers consist of different resins in a state of solution, of which the most common are mastick, sandarach, lack, benzoin, copal, amber, and asphalt. The menstrua are either expressed or essential oils, or spirit of wine.
To varnish; to smear over with lacker, for the purpose of improving color or preserving from tarnishing and decay.
lacquer: A kind of varnish. The basis of lackers is a solution of the substance called seed-lack or shell-lack, in spirit of wine or alcohol. Varnishes applied to metals improve their color and preserve them from tarnishing. Lackers consist of different resins in a state of solution, of which the most common are mastick, sandarach, lack, benzoin, copal, amber, and asphalt. The menstrua are either expressed or essential oils, or spirit of wine.
lackey: An attending servant; a footboy or footman.
To act as footboy; to pay servile attendance. Oft have I servants seen on horses ride, the free and noble lackey by their side.
laconic: Short; brief; pithy; sententious; expressing much in few words, after the manner of the Spartans; as a laconic phrase.
laconical: Short; brief; pithy; sententious; expressing much in few words, after the manner of the Spartans; as a laconic phrase.
laconically: Briefly; concisely; as a sentiment laconically expressed.
laconics: A book of Pausanias, which treats of Lacedemonia.
laconism: A brief sententious phrase or expression.
laconicism: A brief sententious phrase or expression.
lactage: The produce of animals yielding milk.
lactary: Milky; full of white juice like milk.
lactate: In chimistry, a salt formed by the lactic acid, or acid of milk, with a base.
lactation: The act of giving suck; or the time of suckling.
lacteal: Conveying chyle; as a lacteal vessel.
A vessel or slender tube of animal bodies, for conveying chyle from the intestines to the common reservatory.
lacteous: Lacteal; conveying chyle; as a lacteous vessel.
lactescence: Tendency to milk; milkiness or milky color.
In botany, milkiness; the liquor which flows abundantly from a plant, when wounded; commonly white, but sometimes yellow or red.
lactescent: Abounding with a thick colored juice.
lactic: Pertaining to milk, or procured from sour milk or whey; as the lactic acid.
lactiferous: Bearing or conveying milk or white juice; as a lactiferous duct.
Producing a thick colored juice; as a plant.
lacunous: Furrowed or pitted. A lacunose leaf has the disk depressed between the veins.
lacunose: Furrowed or pitted. A lacunose leaf has the disk depressed between the veins.
lad: A young man or boy; a stripling.
ladanum: The resinous juice which exsudes from the leaves of the Cistus ladanifera, a shrub which grows in Arabia, Candia, and other parts of the Archipelago. It is collected with a kind of rake, with leather thongs attached to it, with which the shrubs are brushed. The best sort is in dark-colored black masses, of the consistence of a soft plaster. The other sort is in long rolls coiled up, harder than the former, and of a paler color. It is chiefly used in external applications.
ladder: A frame of wood, consisting of two side pieces, connected by rounds inserted in them at suitable distances, and thus forming steps, by which persons may ascend a building.
That by which a person ascends or rises; means of ascending; as a ladder made of cords. Lowliness is young ambition’s ladder.
Gradual rise; elevation. Mounting fast towards the top of the ladder ecclesiastical.
lade: To load; to put on or in, as a burden or freight. We lade a ship with cotton. We lade a horse or other beast with corn. And they laded their asses with the corn and departed thence. Genesis 42:26.
To dip; to throw in or out, as a fluid, with a ladle or dipper; as, to lade water out of a tub or into a cistern.
The mouth of a river. Obs.
laded: Loaded; charged with a burden or freight.
laden: Loaded; charged with a burden or freight.
lading: That which constitutes a load or cargo; freight; burden; as the lading of a ship. Acts 27:10.
Loading; charging with a burden or freight; throwing or dipping out.
ladle: A utensil somewhat like a dish, with a long handle, used for throwing or dipping out liquor from a vessel.
The receptacle of a mill wheel, which receives the water which moves it.
In gunnery, an instrument for drawing the charge of a cannon.
ladle-ful: The quantity contained in a ladle.
lady: A woman of distinction. Originally, the title of lady was given to the daughters of earls and others in high rank, but by custom, the title belongs to any woman of genteel education.
A word of complaisance; used of women.
Mistress; the female who presides or has authority over a manor or a family.
lady-bird: A small red vaginopennous or sheath-winged insect. A coleopterous insect of the genus Coccinella. LAD Y-BIRD.
lady-bug: A small red vaginopennous or sheath-winged insect. A coleopterous insect of the genus Coccinella. LAD Y-BIRD.
lady-cow: A small red vaginopennous or sheath-winged insect. A coleopterous insect of the genus Coccinella. LAD Y-BIRD.
lady-fly: A small red vaginopennous or sheath-winged insect. A coleopterous insect of the genus Coccinella. LAD Y-BIRD.
lady-day: The day of the annunciation of the holy virgin, March 25th.
lady-like: Like a lady in manners; genteel; well bred.
lag: Coming after or behind; slow; sluggish; tardy.
Last; long delayed; as the lag end.
The lowest class; the rump; the fag end.
To walk or move slowly; to loiter; to stay behind. I shall not lag behind.
lagger: A loiterer; an idler; one who moves slowly and falls behind.
lagging: Loitering; moving slowly and falling behind. The nurse went lagging after with the child.
lagoon: A fen, moor, marsh, shallow pond or lake; as the lagunes of Venice.
lagune: A fen, moor, marsh, shallow pond or lake; as the lagunes of Venice.
laic: Belonging to the laity or people, in distinction from the clergy.
laical: Belonging to the laity or people, in distinction from the clergy.
laid: and pp. of lay; so written for layed.
lain: of lie. Lien would be a more regular orthography, but lain is generally used.
lair: A place of rest; the bed or couch of a boar or wild beast.
laird: In the Scots dialect, a lord; the proprietor of a manor.
laity: The people, as distinguished from the clergy; the body of the people not in orders.
The state of a layman, or of not being in orders.
lake: To play; to sport. North of England. This is play, without a prefix.

A large and extensive collection of water contained in a cavity or hollow of the earth. It differs from a pond in size, the latter being a collection of small extent; but sometimes a collection of water is called a pond or a lake indifferently. North America contains some of the largest lakes on the globe, particularly the lakes Ontario, Erie, Huron, Michigan and Superior.
A middle color between ultramarine and vermilion, made of cochineal.
laky: Pertaining to a lake or lakes.
lama: The sovereign pontiff, or rather the god of the Asiatic Tartars.
A small species of camel, the Camelus lama of South America.
lamantin: A species of the walrus or sea-cow, the Trichechus manatus.
lamentin: A species of the walrus or sea-cow, the Trichechus manatus.
lamb: The young of the sheep kind.
The Lamb of God, in Scripture, the Savior Jesus Christ, who was typified by the paschal lamb. Behold the lamb of God, who taketh away the sin of the world. John 1:29.
To bring forth young, as sheep.
lambative: A medicine taken by licking with the tongue.
lambent: Playing about; touching lightly; gliding over; as a lambent flame.
lamblike: lam'like. Like a lamb; gentle; humble; meek; as a lamblike temper.
lamdoidal: In the form of the Greek A, the English L; as the lamdoidal suture.
lame: Crippled or disabled in a limb, or otherwise injured so as to be unsound and impaired in strength; as a lame arm or leg, or a person lame in one leg.
Imperfect; not satisfactory; as a lame excuse.
Hobbling; not smooth; as numbers in verse.
To make lame; to cripple or disable; to render imperfect and unsound; as, to lame an arm or a leg.
lamel: A thin plate or scale of any thing.
lamellar: Disposed in thin plates or scales.
lamellate: Formed in thin plates or scales, or covered with them.
lamellated: Formed in thin plates or scales, or covered with them.
lamelliferous: Producing plates; an epithet of polypiers presenting lamellar stars, or waved furrows a - garnished’ with plates.
lamelliform: Having the form of a plate.
lamely: Like a cripple; with impaired strength; in a halting manner; as, to walk lamely.
Imperfectly; without a complete exhibition of parts; as a figure lamely drawn; a scene lamely described.
lameness: An impaired state of the body or limbs; loss of natural soundness and strength by a wound or by disease; particularly applied to the limbs, and implying a total or partial inability; as the lameness of the leg or arm.
Imperfection; weakness; as the lameness of an argument or of a description.
lament: To mourn; to grieve; to weep or wail; to express sorrow. Jeremiah lamented for Josiah.2 Chronicles 35:25.
To regret deeply; to feel sorrow.
To bewail; to mourn for; to bemoan; to deplore. One laughed at follies, one lamented crimes.
Grief or sorrow expressed in complaints or cries; lamentation; a weeping. Torment, and loud lament, and furious rage.
lamentable: To be lamented; deserving sorrow; as a lamentable declension of morals.
Mournful; adapted to awaken grief; as a lamentable tune.
Miserable; pitiful; low; poor; in a sense rather ludicrous.
lamentably: Mournfully; with expressions or tokens of sorrow.
lamentation: Expression of sorrow; cries of grief; the act of bewailing. In Rama was there a voice heard, lamentation and weeping. Matthew 2:18.
In the plural, a book of Scripture, containing the lamentations of Jeremiah.
lamenter: One who mourns, or cries out with sorrow.
lamia: A hag; a witch; a demon.
lamin: A thin plate or scale; a layer or coat lying over another; applied to the plates of minerals, bones.
A bone, or part of a bone, resembling a thin plate, such as the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone.
The border, or the upper, broad or spreading part of the petal, in a polypetalous corol.
lamina: A thin plate or scale; a layer or coat lying over another; applied to the plates of minerals, bones.
A bone, or part of a bone, resembling a thin plate, such as the cribriform plate of the ethmoid bone.
The border, or the upper, broad or spreading part of the petal, in a polypetalous corol.
laminable: Capable of being formed into thin plates.
laminar: In plates; consisting of thin plates or layers.
laminate: Plated; consisting of plates, scales or layers, one over another.
laminated: Plated; consisting of plates, scales or layers, one over another.
lamp: A vessel for containing oil to be burned by means of a wick; or a light, a burning wick inserted in a vessel of oil. Hence,

Figuratively, a light of any kind. The moon is called the lamp of heaven. Thy gentle eyes send forth a quickening spirit, to feed the dying lamp of life within me. Lamp of safety, or safety lamp, a lamp for lighting coal mines, without exposing workmen to the explosion of inflammable air.
lampas: A lump of flesh of the size of a nut, in the roof of a horse’s mouth, and rising above the teeth.
lampblack: A fine soot formed by the condensation of the smoke of burning pitch or resinous substances, in a chimney terminating in a cone of cloth .
lampiate: A compound salt, composed of lampic acid and a base.
lampic: The lampic acid is obtained by the combustion of ether by means of a lamp.
lampoon: A personal satire in writing; abuse; censure written to reproach and vex rather than to reform.
To abuse with personal censure; to reproach in written satire.
lampooner: One who abuses with personal satire; the writer of a lampoon. The squibs are those who are called libelers, lampooners, and pamphleteers.
lamprey: A genus of anguilliform fishes, resembling the eel, and moving in water by winding, like the serpent on land. This fish has seven spiracles on each side of the neck, and a fistula or aperture on the top of the head, but no pectoral or ventral fins. The marine or sea lamprey is sometimes found so large as to weigh four or five pounds. Lamprel and lampron.
lanate: Wooly. In botany, covered with a substance like curled hairs; as a lanated leaf or stem.
lanated: Wooly. In botany, covered with a substance like curled hairs; as a lanated leaf or stem.
lance: Il’ans. A spear, an offensive weapon in form of a half pike, used by the ancients and thrown by the hand. It consisted of the shaft or handle, the wings and the dart.
To pierce with a lance or with a sharp pointed instrument. - Seized the due victim, and with fury lanc’d her back.
To pierce or cut; to open with a lancet; as, to lance a vein or an abscess.
lanceolar: In botany, tapering towards each end.
lanceolate: Shaped like a lance; oblong and gradually tapering toward each extremity; spear-shaped; as a lanceolate leaf.
lanceolated: Shaped like a lance; oblong and gradually tapering toward each extremity; spear-shaped; as a lanceolate leaf.
lancer: One who lances; one who carries a lance.
lancet: A surgical instrument, sharp-pointed and two-edged; used in venesection, and in opening tumors, abscesses.
lanch: To throw, as a lance; to dart; to let fly. See whose arm can lanch the surer bolt.
To move, or cause to slide from the land into the water; as, to lanch a ship.
To dart or fly off; to push off; as, to lanch into the wide world; to lanch into a wide field of discussion.
The sliding or movement of a ship from the land into the water, on ways prepared for the purpose.
A kind of boat, longer, lower, and more flat-bottomed than a long boat.
land: Earth, or the solid matter which constitutes the fixed part of the surface of the globe, in distinction from the sea or other waters, which constitute the fluid or movable part. Hence we say, the globe is terraqueous, consisting of land and water. The seaman in a long voyage longs to see land.

Any portion of the solid, superficial part of the globe, whether a kingdom or country, or a particular region. The United States is denominated the land of freedom. Go, view the land, even Jericho. Joshua 2:1.
Any small portion of the superficial part of the earth or ground. We speak of the quantity of land in a manor. Five hundred acres of land is a large farm.
Ground; soil, or the superficial part of the earth in respect to its nature or quality; as good land; poor land; moist or dry land.
Real Estate. A traitor forfeits all his lands and tenements.
landau: A kind of coach or carriage whose top may be opened and thrown back; so called from a town in Germany.
land-breeze: A current of air setting from the land towards the sea.
landed: Disembarked; set on shore from aé ship or boat. 2.a. Having an estate in land; as a landed gentleman. The house of commons must consist, for the most part, of landed men.

Consisting in real estate or land; as landed security; landed property. The landed interest of a nation is the interest consisting in land; but the word is used also for the owners of that interest, the proprietors of land.
landfall: A sudden translation of property in land by the death of a rich man.
In seamen’s language, the first land discovered after a voyage.
landflood: An overflowing of land by water; an inundation. Properly, a flood from the land from the swelling of rivers; but I am not sure that it is always used in this sense.
land-force: A military force, army or troops serving on land, as distinguished from a naval force.
landgrave: In Germany, a count or earl; or an officer nearly corresponding to the earl of England, and the count of France. It is now a title of certain princes who possess estates or territories called landgraviates.
landgraviate: The territory held by a landgrave, or his office, jurisdiction or authority.
landholder: A holder, owner or proprietor of land.
landing: Setting on shore; coming on shore.
A place on the shore of the sea or of a lake, or on the bank of a river; where persons land or come on shore, or where goods are set on shore.
landing-place: A place on the shore of the sea or of a lake, or on the bank of a river; where persons land or come on shore, or where goods are set on shore.
landjobber: A man who makes a business of buying land on speculation, or of buying and selling for the profit of bargains, or who buys and sells for others.
landlady: Awoman who has tenants holding from her.
landless: Destitute of land; having no property in land.
landlock: To inclose or encompass by land.
landlocked: Encompassed by land, so that no point of the compass is open to the sea.
landloper: A landman; literally, a land runner; a term of reproach among seamen to designate a man who passes his life on land.
landlord: The lord of a manor or of land; the owner of land who has tenants under him.
The master of an inn or tavern.
landman: A man who serves on land; opposed to seaman.
landmark: A mark to designate the boundary of land; any mark or fixed object; as a marked tree, a stone, a ditch, or a heap of stones, by which the limits of a farm, a town or other portion of territory may be known and preserved. Thou shalt not remove thy neighbor’s landmark. Deuteronomy 19:14.
In navigation, any elevated object on land that serves as a guide to seamen.
land-office: In the United States, an office in which the sales of new land are registered, and warrants issued for the location of land and other business respecting unsettled land is transacted.
landscape: A portion of land or territory which the eye can comprehend in a single view, including mountains, rivers, lakes, and whatever the land contains. - Whilst the landscape round it measures, russet lawns and fallows gray, where the nibbling flocks do stray.
A picture, exhibiting the form of a district of country, as far as the eye can reach, or a particular extent of land and the objects it contains, or its various scenery.
The view or prospect of a district of country.
landslip: A portion of a hill or mountain, which slips or slides down; or the sliding down of a considerable tract of land from a mountain. landslips are not unfrequent in Swisserland.
landsman: In seaman’s language, a sailor on board a ship, who has not before been at sea.
land-tax: A tax assessed on land and buildings.
land-waiter: An officer of the customs, whose duty is to wait or attend on the landing of goods, and to examine, weigh or measure, and take an account of them.
land-wind: A wind blowing from the land.
lane: A narrow way or passage, or a private passage, as distinguished from a public road or highway. A lane may be open to all passengers, or it may be inclosed and appropriated to a man’s private use. In the United States, the word is used chiefly in the country, and answers in a degree, to an alley in a city. It has sometimes been used for alley. In London, the word lane is added to the names of streets; as chancery lane.
A passage between lines of men, or people standing on each side.
langrage: Langrel shot or langrage, is a particular kind of shot used at sea for tearing sails and rigging, and thus disabling an enemy’s ship. It consists of bolts, nails and other pieces of iron fastened together.
langrel: Langrel shot or langrage, is a particular kind of shot used at sea for tearing sails and rigging, and thus disabling an enemy’s ship. It consists of bolts, nails and other pieces of iron fastened together.
language: Human speech; the expression of ideas by words or significant articulate sounds, for the communication of thoughts. Language consists in the oral utterance of sounds, which usage has made the representatives of ideas. When two or more persons customarily annex the same sounds to the same ideas, the expression of these sounds by one person communicates his ideas to another. This is the primary sense of language, the use of which is to communicate the thoughts of one person to another through the organs of hearing. Articulate sounds are represented by letters, marks or characters which form words. Hence language consists also in .
Words duly arranged in sentences, written, printed or engraved, and exhibited to the eye.
The speech or expression of ideas peculiar to a particular nation. Men had originally one and the same language, but the tribes or families of men, since their dispersion, have distinct languages.
Style; manner of expression. Others for language all their care express.

The inarticulate sounds by which irrational animals express their feelings and wants. Each species of animals has peculiar sounds, which are uttered instinctively, and are understood by its own species, and its own species only.
languaged: Having a language; as many languaged nations.
language-master: One whose profession is to teach languages.
languet: Any thing in the shape of the tongue.
languid: Flagging; drooping; hence, feeble; weak; heavy; dull; indisposed to exertion. The body is languid after excessive action, which exhausts its powers.
Dull; heartless; without animation. And fire their languid soul with Cato’s virtue.
languidness: Weakness from exhaustion of strength; feebleness; dullness; languor.
languish: To lose strength or animation; to be or become dull, feeble or spiritless; to pine; to be or to grow heavy. We languish under disease or after excessive exertion. She that hath borne seven languisheth. Jeremiah 15:9.
To wither; to fade; to lose the vegetating power. For the fields of Heshbon languisheth.Isaiah 16:8.
To grow dull; to be no longer active and vigorous. The war languished for want of supplies. Commerce, agriculture, manufactures languish, not for want of money, but for want of good markets.

To pine or sink under sorrow or any continued passion; as, a woman languishes for the loss of her lover. Therefore shall the land mourn, and every one that dwelleth therein shall languish. Hosea 4:3.&
To look with softness or tenderness, as with the head reclined and a peculiar cast of the eye.
languishing: Becoming or being feeble; losing strength; pining; withering; fading. 2.a. Having a languid appearance; as a languishing eye.
languishment: Softness of look or mien, with the head reclined.
languor: Feebleness; dullness; heaviness; lassitude of body; that state of the body which is induced by exhaustion of strength, as by disease, by extraordinary exertion, by the relaxing effect of heat, or by weakness from any cause./ .
Dullness of the intellectual faculty; listlessness.
Softness; laxity: . To isles of fragrance, lily-silvered vales, diffusing languor in the parting gales.
laniard: lan’yard. A short piece of rope or line, used for fastening something in ships, as the laniards of the gun-ports, of the buoy, of the cathook, but especially used to extend the shrouds and stays of the masts, by their communication with the dead eyes.
lank: Loose or lax and easily yielding to pressure; not distended; not stiff or firm by distension; not plump; as a lank bladder or purse. The clergy’s bags are lank and lean with thy extortions.
Thin; slender; meager; not full and firm; as a lank body.
lantern: A case or vessel made of tin perforated with many holes, or of some transparent substance, as glass, horn, or oiled paper; used for carrying a candle or other light in the open air, or into stables. A dark lantern is one with a single opening, which may be closed so as to conceal the light.
A light-house or light to direct the course of ships.
In architecture, a little dome raised over the roof of a building to give light, and to serve as a crowning to the fabric.

A square cage of carpentry placed over the ridge of a corridor or gallery, between two rows of shops, to illuminate them. Magic lantern, an optical machine by which painted images are represented so much magnified as to appear like the effect of magic.
lantern-fly: An insect of the genus Fulgora.
lanuginous: Downy; covered with down, or fine’ soft hair.
laodicean: Like the christians of Laodicea: lukewarm in religion.
lap: The loose part of a coat; the lower part of a garment that plays loosely.
The part of clothes that lies on the knees when a person sits down; hence, the knees in this position. Men expect that happiness should drop into their laps.
To fold; to bend and lay over or on; as, to lap a piece of cloth. To lap boards, is to lay one partly over another.
To wrap or twist round. I lapped a slender thread about the paper.
To infold; to involve. Her garment spreads, and laps him in the folds.
lapdog: A small dog fondled in the lap.
lapfull: As much as the lap can contain. 2 Kings 4:39.
lapidary: An artificer who cuts precious stones.
A virtuoso skilled in the nature and kinds of gems or precious stones.
Pertaining to the art of cutting stones. The lapidary style denotes that which is proper for monumental and other inscriptions.
lapidation: The act of stoning a person to death.
lapideous: Stony; of the nature of stone; as lapideous matter.
lapidescence: The process of becoming stone; a hardening into a stony substance.
lapidescent: Growing or turning to stone; that has the quality of petrifying bodies.
Any substance which has the quality of petrifying a body, or converting it to stone.
lapidification: The operation of forming or converting into a stony substance, by means of a liquid charged with earthy particles in solution, which crystallize in the interstices, and end in forming free stone, pudding stone
lapidify: To turn into stone; to become stone.
lapis: in Latin, a stone. Hence, Lapis Bononiensis, the Bolognian stone. Lapis Hepaticus, liver stone. Lapis Lazuli, azure stone, an aluminous mineral, of a rich blue color, resembling the blue carbonate of copper. ] . Lapis Lydius, touch-stone; basanite; a variety of siliceous slate.
lapper: One that laps; one that wraps or folds.
One that takes up with his tongue.
lappet: A part of a garment or dress that hangs loose.
lapping: Licking; taking into the mouth with the tongue.&
lapse: A sliding, gliding or flowing; a smooth course; as the lapse of a stream; the lapse of time.
A falling or passing. The lapse to indolence is soft and imperceptible, but the return to diligence is difficult.

A slip an error; a fault; a failing in duty; a slight deviation from truth or rectitude. This Scripture may be usefully applied as a caution to guard against those lapses and fallings to which our infirmities daily expose us. So we say, a lapse in style or propriety.
In ecclesiastical law, the slip or omission of a patron to present a clerk to a benefice, within six months after it becomes void. In this case, the benefice is said to be lapsed, or in lapse.
lapsed: Fallen; passed from one proprietor to another by the negligence of the patron; as a lapsed benefice. A lapsed legacy is one which falls to the heirs through the failure of the legatee, as when the legatee dies before the testator.
lapsided: Having one side heavier than the other, as a ship.
lapsing: Gliding; flowing; failing; falling to one person through the omission of another.
lapwing: A bird of the genus Tringa; the tewit.
lapwork: Work in which one part laps over another.
larboard: The left hand side of a ship, when a person stands with his face to the head; opposed to starboard.
Pertaining to the left hand side of a ship; as the larboard quarter.
larceny: Theft; the act of taking and carrying away the goods or property of another feloniously. Larceny is of two kinds; simple larceny, or theft, not accompanied with any atrocious circumstance; and mixed or compound larceny, which includes in it the aggravation of taking from one’s house or person, as in burglary or robbery. The stealing of any thing below the value of twelve pence, is called petty larceny; above that value, it is called grand larceny.
larch: The common name of a division of the genus Pinus, species of which are natives of America, as well as of Europe.
lard: The fat of swine, after being melted and separated from the flesh.
To stuff with bacon or pork. The larded thighs on loaded altars laid.
To fatten; to enrich. Now Falstaff sweats to death, and lards the lean earth.
To mix with something by way of improvement. - Let no alien interpose, to lard with wit thy hungry Epsom prose.
lardaceous: Of the nature of lard; consisting of lard.
larder: A room where meat is kept or salted.
large: Big; of great size; bulky; as a large body; a large horse or ox; a large mountain; a large tree; a large ship.
Wide; extensive; as a large field or plain; a large extent of territory.
Extensive or populous; containing many inhabitants; as a large city or town.
Abundant; plentiful; ample; as a large supply of provisions.
Copious; diffusive. I might be very large on the importance and advantages of education.
largely: Copiously; diffusely; amply. The subject was largely discussed.
Liberally; bountifully. - How he lives and eats; how largely gives.
Abundantly. They their fill of love and love’s disport took largely.
largeness: Bigness; bulk; magnitude; as the largeness of an animal.
Greatness; comprehension; as the largeness of mind or of capacity.
Extent; extensiveness; as largeness of views.
Extension; amplitude; liberality; as the largeness of an offer; largeness of heart.
Wideness; extent; as the largeness of a river.
largess: A present; a gift or donation; a bounty bestowed.
largo: Musical terms, directing to slow movement. Large is one degree quicker than grave, and two degrees quicker than adagio.
larghetto: Musical terms, directing to slow movement. Large is one degree quicker than grave, and two degrees quicker than adagio.
lark: A bird of the genus Alauda, distinguished for its singing.
larkspur: A plant of the genus Delphinium.
larmier: The flat jutting part of a cornice; literally, the dropper; the eave or drip of a house.
larum: Alarm; a noise giving notice of danger.
larva: An insect in the caterpillar state; eruca; the state of an insect when the animal is masked, and before it has attained its winged or perfect state; the first stage in the metamorphoses of insects, preceding the chrysalis and perfect insect.
larve: An insect in the caterpillar state; eruca; the state of an insect when the animal is masked, and before it has attained its winged or perfect state; the first stage in the metamorphoses of insects, preceding the chrysalis and perfect insect.
larvated: Masked; clothed as with a mask.
laryngotomy: The operation of cutting the larynx or windpipe; the making of an incision into the larynx for assisting respiration when obstructed, or removing foreign bodies; bronchotomy; tracheotomy.
larynx: In anatomy, the upper part of the windpipe or trachea, a cartilaginous cavity, which modulates the voice in speaking and singing.
lascar: In the East Indies, a native seaman, or a gunner.
lascivious: Loose; wanton; lewd; lustful; as lascivious men; lascivious desires; lascivious eyes.
Soft; wanton; luxurious. LASC IOUS. He capers numbly in a lady’s chamber, to the lascivious pleasing of a lute.
lasciviousness: Looseness; irregular indulgence of animal desires; wantonness; lustfulness. Who, being past feeling, have given themselves over to lasciviousness. Ephesians 4:19.
Tendency to excite lust, and promote irregular indulgences. The reason pretended by Augustus was, the lasciviousness of his Elegies and his Art of Love.
lash: The thong or braided cord of a whip. I observed that your whip wanted a lash to it.
A stroke with a whip, or any thing pliant and tough. The culprit was whipped thirty nine lashes.
A stroke of satire; a sarcasm; an expression or retort that cuts or gives pain. The moral is a lash at the vanity of arrogating that to ourselves which succeeds well.
To strike with a lash or any thing pliant; to whip or scourge. We lash the pupil and defraud the ward.
lashed: Struck with a lash; whipped; tied; made fast by a rope.
lasher: A piece of rope for binding or making fast one thing to another.
lashing: A piece of rope for binding or making fast one thing to another.
lassitude: Weakness; dullness; heaviness; weariness; languor of body or mind, proceeding from exhaustion of strength by excessive labor or action, or other means.
Among physicians, lassitude is a morbid sensation or languor which often precedes disease, in which case it proceeds from an impaired or diseased action of the organs.
lasslorn: Forsaken by his lass or mistress.
last: That comes after all the others; the latest; applied to time; as the last hour of the day; the last day of the year.
That follows all the others; that is behind all the others in place; hindmost; as, this was the last man that entered the church.
Beyond which there is no more. Here, last of Britons, let your names be read.
Next before the present; as the last week; the last year.
Utmost. Their last endeavors bend, T’ outshine each other. It is an object of the last importance.
lastage: A duty paid for freight or transportation.
lasting: a. Durable; of long continuance; that may continue or endure; as a lasting good or evil; a lasting color.
lastingness: Durability; the quality or state of long continuance.
lastly: In the conclusion; at last; finally.
latch: A small piece of iron or wood used to fasten a door.
A small line like a loop, used to lace the bonnets to the courses, or the drabblers to the bonnets.
To fasten with a latch; to fasten.
latchet: The string that fastens a shoe. Mark 1:7.
late: Coming after the usual time; slow; tardy; long delayed; as a late spring; a late summer. The crops or harvest will be late.
Far advanced towards the end or close; as a late hour of the day. He began at a late period of his life.
Last, or recently in any place, office or character; as the late ministry; the late administration.
Existing not long ago, but now decayed or departed; as the late bishop of London.
Not long past; happening not long ago; recent; as the late rains. We have received late intelligence.
lateen: A lateen sail is a triangular sail, extended by a lateen yard, which is slung about one quarter the distance from the lower end, which is brought down at the tack while the other end is elevated at an angle of about 45 degrees; used in xebecs, polacres and settees, in the Mediterranean.
lately: Not long ago; recently. We called on a gentleman who has lately arrived from Italy.
latency: The state of being concealed; abstruseness.
lateness: The state of being tardy, or of coming after the usual time; as the lateness of spring or of harvest.
Time far advanced in any particular period; as lateness of the day or night; lateness in the season; lateness in life.
The state of being out of time, or after the appointed time; as the lateness of one’s arrival.
latent: Hid; concealed; secret; not seen; not visible or apparent. We speak of latent motives; latent reasons; latent springs of action. Latent heat, is heat in combination, in distinction from sensible heat; the portion of heat which disappears, when a body changes its form from the solid to the fluid, or from the fluid to the aeriform state.
lateral: Pertaining to the side; as the lateral view of an object.
Proceeding from the side; as the lateral branches of a tree; lateral shoots.
laterality: The quality of having distinct sides.
laterally: In the direction of the side.
lateran: One of the churches at Rome. The name is said to have been derived from that of a man. A latere, A legate a latere, is a pope’s legate or envoy, so called because sent from his side, from among his favorites and counselors.
laterifolious: In botany, growing on the side of a leaf at the base; as a laterifolious flower.
lateritious: Like bricks; of the color of bricks. Lateritious sediment, a sediment in urine resembling brick dust, observed after the crises of fevers, and at the termination of gouty paroxysms.
lath: A thin, narrow board or slip of wood nailed to the rafters of a building to support the tiles or covering.
A thin narrow slip of wood nailed to the studs, to support the plastering.
To cover or line with laths.

In some parts of England, a part or division of a county. Spenser, Spelman and Blackstone do not agree in their accounts of the lath; but according to the laws of Edward the Confessor, the lath, in some counties, answered to the trithing or third part of a county in others.
lathe: An engine by which instruments of wood, ivory, metals and other materials, are turned and cut into a smooth round form.
lather: To form a foam with water and soap; to become froth, or frothy matter.
To spread over with the foam of soap.
Foam or froth made by soap moistened with water.
Foam or froth from profuse sweat, as of a horse.
lathy: Thin as a lath; long and slender.
latibulize: To retire into a den, burrow or cavity, and lie dormant in winter; to retreat and lie hid. The tortoise latibulizes in October.
laticlave: An ornament of dress worn by Roman senators. It is supposed to have been a broad stripe of purple on the fore part of the tunic, set with knobs or studs.
latin: Pertaining to the Latins, a people of Latium, in Italy; Roman; as the Latin language. Latin church, the western church; the christian church in Italy, France, Spain and other countries where the Latin language was introduced, as distinct from the Greek or eastern church.
The language of the ancient Romans.
An exercise in schools, consisting in turning English into Latin.
latinism: A Latin idiom; a mode of speech peculiar to the Latins.
latinity: Purity of the Latin style or idiom; the Latin tongue.
latinize: To give to foreign words Latin terminations and make them Latin.
To use words or phrases borrowed from the Latin.
latirostrous: Having a broad beak, as a fowl.
latitancy: The state of lying concealed; the state of lurking.
latitat: A writ by which a person is summoned into the king’s bench to answer, as supposing he lies concealed.
latitude: Breadth; width; extent from side to side.
In astronomy, the distance of a star north or south of the ecliptic.
In geography, the distance of any place on the globe, north or south of the equator. Boston is situated in the forty third degree of north latitude.
Extent of meaning or construction; indefinite acceptation. The words will not bear this latitude of construction.
latitudinal: Pertaining to latitude; in the direction of latitude.
latitudinarian: Not restrained; not confined by precise limits; free; thinking or acting at large; as latitudinarian opinions or doctrines.
One who is moderate in his notions, or not restrained by precise settled limits in opinion; one who indulges freedom in thinking.
In theology, one who departs in opinion from the strict principles of orthodoxy; or one who indulges a latitude of thinking and interpretation; a moderate man.
latitudinarianism: Freedom or liberality of opinion, particularly in theology.
latria: The highest kind of worship, or that paid to God; distinguished by the Catholics from dulia, or the inferior worship paid to saints.
latrobite: A newly described mineral of a pale pink red color, massive or crystallized, from an isle near the Labrador coast.
latten-brass: Plates of milled brass reduced to different thicknesses, according to the uses they are intended for.
latter: Coming or happening after something else; opposed to former; as the former and latter rain; former or latter harvest.
Mentioned the last of two. The difference between reason and revelation - and in what sense the latter is superior.
Modern; lately done or past; as in these latter ages.
latterly: Of late; in time not long past; lately.
lattermath: The latter mowing; that which is mowed after a former mowing.
lattice: Any work of wood or iron, made by crossing laths, rods or bars, and forming open squares like net-work; as the lattice of a window. The mother of Sisera looked out at a window, and cried through the lattice. Judges 5:28.
Consisting of cross pieces; as lattice work.
Furnished with lattice work; as a lattice window.
To form with cross bars, and open work.
laud: Praise; commendation; an extolling in words; honorable mention.
That part of divine worship which consists in praise.
Music or singing in honor of any one.
To praise in words alone, or with words and singing; to celebrate.
laudable: Praiseworthy; commendable; as laudable motives; laudable actions.
Healthy; salubrious; as laudable juices of the body.
Healthy; well digested; as laudable pus.
laudableness: The quality of deserving praise; praiseworthiness; as the laudableness of designs, purposes, motives or actions.
laudanum: Opium dissolved in spirit or wine; tincture of opium.
laugh: To make the noise and exhibit the features which are characteristic of mirth in the human species. Violent laughter is accompanied with a shaking of the sides, and all laughter expels breath from the lungs.

In poetry, to be gay; to appear gay, cheerful, pleasant, lively or brilliant. Then laughs the childish year with flow’rets crown’d. And o’er the foaming bowl, the laughing wine. To laugh at, to ridicule; to treat with some degree of contempt. No fool to laugh at, which he valued more. To laugh to scorn, to deride; to treat with mockery, contempt and scorn. Nehemiah 2:19.
l’aff. An expression of mirth peculiar to the human species. But feigns a laugh, to see me search around, and by that laugh the willing fair is found.
laughable: l’affable. That may justly excite laughter; as a laughable story; a laughable scene.
laugher: l’affer. One who laughs, or is fond of merriment. The laughers are a majority.
laughing: l’affing. Expressing mirth in a particular manner.
laughingly: l’affingly. In a merry way; with laughter.
laughing-stock: An object of ridicule; a butt of sport.
laughter: l’affter. Convulsive merriment; an expression of mirth peculiar to man, consisting in a peculiar noise and configuration of features, with a shaking of the sides and expulsion of breath.

Convulsive merriment; an expression of mirth peculiar to man, consisting in a peculiar noise and configuration of features, with a shaking of the sides and expulsion of breath. I said of laughter, it is mad. Ecclesiastes 2:2.
laumonite: Efflorescent zeolite; so called from Laumont, its discoverer. It is found in laminated masses, in groups of prismatic crystals or prismatic distinct concretions. Exposed to the air, it disintegrates.
launder: A washer-woman; also, a long and hollow trough, used by miners to receive the powdered ore from the box where it is beaten.
launderer: l’anderer. A man who follows the business of washing clothes.
laundress: l’andress. A washer-woman; a female whose employment is to wash clothes.
laundry: The place or room where clothes are washed.
laureate: Decked or invested with laurel; as laureate hearse. Soft on her lap her laureate son reclines. Poet laureate, in Great Britain, an officer of the king’s household, whose business is to compose an ode annually for the king’s birthday, and for the new year. It is said this title was first given him in the time of Edward IV.
To honor with a degree in the university, and a present of a wreath of laurel.
laureated: Honored with a degree and a laurel wreath.
laureation: The act of conferring a degree in the university, together with a wreath of laurel; an honor bestowed on those who excelled in writing verse. This was an ancient practice at Oxford, from which probably originated the denomination of poet laureate.
laurel: The bay-tree or Laurus, a genus of plants of several species. ( .
laureled: Crowned or decorated with laurel, or with a laurel wreath; laureate.
laurustin: A plant of the genus Viburnum, an evergreen shrub or tree, whose flowers are said to continue through the winter.
lauskraut: A plant of the genus Delphinium.
lautu: A band of cotton, twisted and worn on the head of the Inca of Peru, as a badge of royalty.
lava: A mass or stream of melted minerals or stony matter which bursts or is thrown from the mouth or sides of a volcano, and is sometimes ejected in such quantities as to overwhelm cities. Catana, at the foot of Etna, has often been destroyed by it, and in 1783, a vast tract of land in Iceland was overspread by an eruption of lava from mount Hecla.
The same matter when cool and hardened.
lavatory: Awash or lotion for a diseased part.
A place where gold is obtained by washing.
lave: To wash; to bathe; a word used chiefly in poetry or rhetoric.
To bathe; to wash one’s self.
To throw up or out; to lade out.
laveer: In seamen’s language, to tack; to sail back and forth.
lavender: A plant, or a genus of aromatic plants, Lavandula.
laver: A vessel for washing; a large basin; in scripture history, a basin placed in the court of the Jewish tabernacle, where the officiating priests washed their hands and feet and the entrails of victims.
lavish: Prodigal; expending or bestowing with profusion; profuse. He was lavish of expense; lavish of praise; lavish of encomiums; lavish of censure; lavish of blood and treasure.
Wasteful; expending without necessity; liberal to a fault.
Wild; unrestrained. Curbing his lavish spirit.
To expend or bestow with profusion; as, to lavish praise or encomiums.
To waste; to expend without necessity or use; to squander; as, to lavish money on vices and amusements.
lavishing: Expending or laying out with profusion; wasting.
lavolta: An old dance in which was much turning and capering.
law: A rule, particularly an established or permanent rule, prescribed by the supreme power of a state to its subjects, for regulating their actions, particularly their social actions. Laws are imperative or mandatory, commanding what shall be done; prohibitory, restraining from what is to be forborn; or permissive, declaring what may be done without incurring a penalty. The laws which enjoin the duties of piety and morality, are prescribed by God and found in the Scriptures. Law is beneficence acting by rule.

Municipal law, is a rule of civil conduct prescribed by the supreme power of a state, commanding what its subjects are to do, and prohibiting what they are to forbear; a statute. Municipal or civil laws are established by the decrees, edicts or ordinances of absolute princes, as emperors and kings, or by the formal acts of the legislatures of free states. Law therefore is sometimes equivalent to decree, edict, or ordinance.

Law of nature, is a rule of conduct arising out of the natural relations of human beings established by the Creator, and existing prior to any positive precept. Thus it is a law of nature, that one man should not injure another, and murder and fraud would be crimes, independent of any prohibition from a supreme power.

Laws of animal nature, the inherent principles by which the economy and functions of animal bodies are performed, such as respiration, the circulation of the blood, digestion, nutrition, various secretions.
Laws of vegetation, the principles by which plats are produced, and their growth carried on till they arrive to perfection.
lawful: Agreeable to law; conformable to law; allowed by law; legal; legitimate. That is deemed lawful which no law forbids, but many things are lawful which are not expedient.
Constituted by law; rightful; as the lawful owner of lands.
lawfully: Legally; in accordance with law; without violating law. We may lawfully do what the laws do not forbid.
lawfulness: The quality of being conformable to law; legality. The lawfulness of an action does not always prove its propriety or expedience.
lawgiver: One who makes or enacts a law; a legislator.
lawing: Expeditation; the act of cutting off the claws and balls of the fore feet of mastiffs to prevent them from running after deer.
lawless: Not subject to law; unrestrained by law; as a lawless tyrant; lawless men.
Contrary to law; illegal; unauthorized; as a lawless claim. He needs no indirect nor lawless course.
Not subject to the ordinary laws of nature; uncontrolled. He, meteor-like, flames lawless through the void.
lawlessly: In a manner contrary to law.
lawlessness: The quality or state of being unrestrained by law; disorder.
law-maker: One who enacts or ordains laws; a legislator; a lawgiver. Law-makers should not be law-breakers.
law-monger: A low dealer in law; a pettifogger.
lawn: An open space between woods, or a plain in a park or adjoining a noble seat. Betwixt them lawns or level downs, and flocks grazing the tender herbs, were interspers’d.
A sort of fine linen. Its use in the sleeves of bishops, explains the following line. A saint in crape is twice a saint in lawn.
lawny: Level, as a plain; like a lawn.
lawsuit: a suit in law for the recovery of a supposed right; a process in law instituted by a party to compel another to do him justice.
lawyer: One versed in the laws, or a practitioner of law; one whose profession is to institute suits in courts of law, and to prosecute or defend the cause of clients. this is a general term, comprehending attorneys, counselors, solicitors, barristers, serjeants and advocates.
lax: Loose; flabby; soft; not tense, firm or rigid; as lax flesh; a lax fiber.
Slack; not tight or tense; as a lax cord.
Not firly united; of loose texture; as gravel and the like laxer matter.
Not rigidly exact; as a lax moral discourse.
laxation: The act of loosening or slackening; or the state of being loose or slackened.
laxative: Having the power or quality of loosening or opening the bowels, and relieving from constipation.
A medicine that relaxes the bowels and relieves from costiveness; a gentle purgative.
laxity: Looseness; slackness; the opposite of tenseness or tension.
Want of exactness or precision; as laxity of expression.
Looseness; defect of exactness; as laxity of morals.
Looseness, as of the bowels; the opposite of costiveness.
laxness: Looseness; softness; flabbiness; as the laxness of flesh or of muscles.
Looseness, as of morals or discipline.
lay: of lie. The estate lay in the county of Hartford. When Ahab heard these words, he rent his clothes, and put sackcloth upon his head, and fasted and lay in sackcloth. 7 Kings 21:27.

Literally, to throw down; hence, to put or place; applied to things broad or long, and in this respect differing from set. We lay a book on the table, when we place it on its side, but we set it on the end. We lay the foundation of a house, but we set a building on its foundation. He laid his robe from him. Jonah 3:6. Soft on the flowery herb I found me laid. A stone was brought and laid on the mouth of the den. Daniel 6:17.
To beat down; to prostrate. Violent winds with rain lay corn and grass.
To settle; to fix and keep from rising. A shower lays the dust.
lay-clerk: A vocal officiate in a cathedral.
layer: A stratum; a bed; a body spread over another; as a layer of clay or of sand.
layman: Aman who is not a clergyman; one of the laity or people, distinct from the clergy.
An image used by painters in contriving attitudes.
laystall: A heap of dung, or a place where dung is laid.
lazar: A person infected with nauseous and pestilential disease.
lazaret: A public building, hospital or pest-house for the reception of diseased persons, particularly for those affected with contagious distempers.
lazaretto: A public building, hospital or pest-house for the reception of diseased persons, particularly for those affected with contagious distempers.
lazar-house: A lazaretto; also, a hospital for quarantine.
lazarwort: Laserpitium, a genus of plants of several species, natives of Germany, Italy, France
laserwort: Laserpitium, a genus of plants of several species, natives of Germany, Italy, France
lazily: In a heavy, sluggish manner; sluggishly. Whether he lazily and listlessly dreams away his time.
laziness: The state or quality of being lazy; indisposition to action or exertion; indolence; sluggishness; heaviness in motion; habitual sloth. Laziness differs from idleness; the latter being a mere defect or cessation of action, but laziness is sloth, with natural or habitual disinclination to action. Laziness travels so slowly, that poverty soon overtakes him.
lazuli: Lapis Lazuli is a mineral of a fine, azure blue color, usually amorphous, or in rounded masses of a moderate size. It is often marked by yellow spots or veins of sulphuret of iron, and is much valued for ornamental work. It is distinguished from lazulite, by the intenseness of its color.
lazulite: A mineral of a light, indigo blue color, occurring in small masses, or crystallized in oblique four-sided prisms.
lazy: Disinclined to action or exertion; naturally or habitually slothful; sluggish; indolent; averse to labor; heavy in motion. Wicked men will ever live like rogues, and not fall to work, but be lazy and spend victuals.
Slow; moving slowly or apparently with labor; as a lazy stream. The night-owl’s lazy flight.
lea: A meadow or plain. The Welsh write lle, but as this word is from the root of lay, the latter is the more correct orthography.
ley: A meadow or plain. The Welsh write lle, but as this word is from the root of lay, the latter is the more correct orthography.
a different orthography of lay and lea, a meadow or field.
leach: To wash, as ashes, by percolation, or causing water to pass through them, and thus to separate from them the alkali. The water thus charged with alkali, is called lye.
A quantity of wood ashes, through which water passes, and thus imbibes the alkali.
leach-tub: A wooden vessel or tub in which ashes are leached. It is sometimes written letch-tub.
lead: A metal of a dull white color, with a cast of blue. It is the least elastic and sonorous of all the metals, and at the same time it is soft and easily fusible. It is found native in small masses, but generally mineralized by sulphur, and sometimes by other substances. Lead fused in a strong heat, throws off vapors which are unwholesome.
A plummet or mass of lead, used in sounding at sea.
Leads, a flat roof covered with lead. och White lead, the oxyd of lead, ground with one third part of chalk.
led. To cover with lead; to fit with lead.
leaden: Made of lead; as a leaden ball.
leader: One that leads or conducts; a guide; a conductor.
A chief; a commander; a captain.
The chief of a party or faction; as the leader of the whigs or of the tories; a leader of the Jacobins.
a performer who leads a band or choir in music.
leading: Guiding; conducting; preceding; drawing; alluring; passing life.
a. Chief; principal; capital; most influential; as a leading motive; a leading man in a party.
showing the way by going first. He left his mother a countess by patent, which was a new leading example.
Guidance; the act of conducting; direction.
leading-strings: Strings by which children are supported when beginning to walk. To be in leading strings, to be in a state of infancy or dependence, or in pupilage under the guidance of others.
leadman: One who begins or leads a dance. Obs.
leadwort: led’wort. Plumbago, a genus of plants.
leady: led’dy. Of the color of lead.
leaf: In botany, leaves are organs of perspiration and inhalation in plants. They usually shoot from the sides of the stems and branches, but sometimes from the root; sometimes they are sessile; more generally supported by petioles. They are of various forms, flat, extended, linear, cylindric.
The thin, extended part of a flower; a petal.
A part of a book containing two pages.
The side of a double door. 7 Kings 6:34.
Something resembling a leaf in thinness and extension; a very thin plate; as gold leaf.
leafless: Destitute of leaves; as a leafless tree.
leaflet: In botany, one of the divisions of a compound leaf; a foliole.
leaf-stalk: The petiole or stalk which supports a leaf.
leafy: Full of leaves; as the leafy forest.
league: An alliance or confederacy between princes or states for their mutual aid or defense; a national contract or compact. A league may be offensive or defensive, or both. It is offensive, when the contracting parties agree to unite in attacking a common enemy; defensive, when the parties agree to act in concert in defending each other against an enemy.

A combination or union of two or more parties for the purpose of maintaining friendship and promoting their mutual interest, or for executing any design in concert. And let there be ‘twixt us and them no league, nor amity.
To unite, as princes or states in a contract of amity for mutual aid or defense; to confederate. Russia and Austria leagued to oppose the ambition of Buonaparte.
leagued: lee’ged. United in mutual compact; confederated.
leaguer: lee’ger. One who unites in a league; a confederate.
Siege; investment of a town or fort by an army.
leak: A crack, crevice, fissure or hole in a vessel, that admits water, or permits a fluid to escape.

The oozing or passing of water or other fluid or liquor through a crack, fissure or aperture in a vessel, either into it, as into a ship, or out of it, as out of a cask. To spring a leak, is to open or crack so as to let in water; to being to let in water.

To let water or other liquor into or out of a vessel, through a hole or crevice in the vessel. A ship leaks, when she admits water through her seams or an aperture in her bottom or sides, into the hull. A pail or a cask leaks, when it admits liquor to pass out through a hole or crevice. To lead out, to find vent; to escape privately from confinement or secrecy; as a fact or report.
leakage: A leaking; or the quantity of a liquor that enters or issues by leaking.
An allowance, in commerce, of a certain rate per cent, for the leaking of casks, or the waste of liquors by leaking.
leaky: That admits water or other liquor to pass in or out; as a leaky vessel; a leaky ship or barrel.
Apt to disclose secrets; tattling; not close.
leamer: A dog; a kind of hound.
lean: To deviate or move from a straight or perpendicular line; or to be in a position thus deviating. We say, a column leans to the north or to the east; it leans to the right or left.
To incline or propend; to tend toward. They delight rather to lean to their old customs - .
To bend or incline so as to rest on something; as, to lean against a wall or a pillar; to lean on the arm of another.
To bend; to be in a bending posture.
To incline; to cause to lean.
leanness: Destitution of fat; want of flesh; thinness of body; meagerness; applied to animals.
Want of matter; poverty; emptiness; as the leanness of a purse.
In Scripture, want of grace and spiritual comfort. He sent leanness into’ their soul.Psalm 106:15.
leap: To spring or rise from the ground with both feet, as man, or with all the feet, as other animals; to jump; to vault; as, a man leaps over a fence, or leaps upon a horse. A man leapeth better with weights in his hands than without.
To spring or move suddenly; as, to leap from a horse.
To rush with violence. And the man in whom the evil spirit was, leaped on them and overcame them - Acts 19:16.
To spring; to bound; to skip; as, to leap for joy.
To fly; to start. Job 41:19. He parted frowning from me, as if ruin leaped from his eyes. [Our common people retain the Saxon aspirate of this word in the phrase, to clip it, to run fast.I .
leaper: One that leaps. A horse is called a good leaper.
leap-frog: A play of children in which they imitate the leap of frogs.
leap-year: Blissextile, a year containing 366 days; every fourth year, which leaps over a day more than a common year. Thus in common years, if the first day of March is on Monday, the present year, it will, the next year, fall on Tuesday, but in leap-year it will leap to Wednesday; for leap-year contains a day more than a common year, a day being added to the month of February.
learn: To gain knowledge of; to acquire knowledge or ideas of something before unknown. We learn the use of letters, the meaning of words and the principles of science. We learn things by instruction, by study, and by experience and observation. It is much easier to learn what is right, than to unlearn what is wrong. Now learn a parable of the fig tree. Matthew 24:32.
To acquire skill in any thing; to gain by practice a faculty of performing; as, to learn to play on a flute or an organ. The chief art of learning is to attempt but little at a time.
To teach; to communicate the knowledge of something before unknown. Hast thou not learned me how to make perfumes?
To gain or receive knowledge; to receive instruction; to take pattern; with of. Take my yoke upon you, and learn of me; for I am meek and lowly - Matthew 11:29.
learned: Versed in literature and science; as a learned man.
Skillful; well acquainted with arts; knowing; within; as learned in martial arts.
Containing learning; as a learned treatise or publication.
learnedly: lern’edly. With learning or erudition; with skill; as, to discuss a question learnedly. Every coxcomb swears as learnedly as they.
learner: lern’er. A person who is gaining knowledge from instruction, from reading or study, or by other means; one who is in the rudiments of any science or art.
learning: lern’ing. Gaining knowledge by instruction or reading, by study, by experience or observation; acquiring skill by practice.

The knowledge of principles or facts received by instruction or study; acquired knowledge or ideas in any branch of science or literature; erudition; literature; science. The Scaligers were men of great learning.
Knowledge acquired by experience, experiment or observation.
Skill in anything good or bad.
lease: A demise or letting of lands, tenements or hereditaments to another for life, for a term of years, or at will, for a rent or compensation reserved; also, the contract for such letting.
Any tenure by grant or permission. Our high placed Macbeth shall live the lease of nature.
To let; to demise; to grant the temporary possession of lands, tenements or hereditaments to another for a rent reserved. A leased to B his land in Dale for the annual rent of a pepper corn.
leez. To glean; to gather what harvest men have left. Obs.
leased: Demised or let, as lands or tenements.
leasehold: Held by lease; as a leasehold tenement.
leaser: A gleaner; a gatherer after reapers.
leash: A thong of leather, or long line by which a falconer holds his hawk, or a courser his dog.
Among sportsmen, a brace and a half; tierce; three; three creatures of any kind, especially greyhounds, foxes, bucks and hares.
A band wherewith to tie any thing.
To bind; to hold by a string.
least: Smallest; little beyond others, either in size or degree; as the least insect; the least mercy. Least is often used without the noun to which it refers. “l am the least of the apostles,” that is, the least apostle of all the apostles. Corinthians 15:9.

In the smallest or lowest degree; in a degree below all others; as, to reward those who least deserve it. At least, At the least, To say no more; not to demand or affirm more than is barely sufficient; at the lowest degree. If he has not incurred a penalty, he at least deserves censure. He who tempts, though vain, at least asperses the tempted with dishonor.

To say no more. Let useful observations be at least a part of your conversation. The least, in the smallest degree. His faculties are not in the least impaired. At leastwise, in the sense of at least, is obsolete.
leasy: s as z. Thin; flimsy. It is usually pronounced sleazy.
leat: A trench to conduct water to or from a mill.
leather: The skin of an animal dressed and prepared for use.
Lethern; consisting of leather; as a leather glove.
lether: The skin of an animal dressed and prepared for use.
Lethern; consisting of leather; as a leather glove.
leather-coat: An apple with a tough coat or rind.
leather-dresser: One who dresses leather; one who prepares hides for use.
leather-jacket: A fish of the Pacific ocean.
leather-mouthed: By leather-mouthed fish, I mean such as have their teeth in their throat, as the chub. LEA THER-MOUTHED.
leathern: Made of leather; consisting of leather; as a lethern purse; a lethern girdle.
lethern: Made of leather; consisting of leather; as a lethern purse; a lethern girdle.
leather-seller: A seller or dealer in leather.
lether-seller: A seller or dealer in leather.
leave: Permission; allowance; license; liberty granted by which restraint or illegality is removed. No friend has leave to bear away the dead. David earnestly asked leave of me. 7 Samuel 20:6, 28.
Farewell; adieu; ceremony of departure; a formal parting of friends; used chiefly in the phrase to take leave. Acis 18:78.

To withdraw or depart from; to quit for a longer or shorter time indefinitely, or for perpetuity. We left Cowes on our return to the United States, May 10, 1825. We leave home for a day or a year. The fever leaves the patient daily at a certain hour. The secretary has left the business of his office with his first clerk. A man shall leave his father and his mother, and cleave to his wife. Genesis 2:24.
To forsake; to desert; to abandon; to relinquish. We have left all and followed thee. Mark 10:28.
leaved: Having a leaf, or made with leaves or folds; as a two-leaved gate.
leaven: A mass of sour dough, which, mixed with a larger quantity of dough or paste, produces fermentation in it and renders it light. During the seven days of the passover, no leaven was permitted to be in the houses of the Jews. Exodus 12:15, 19.

Any thing which makes a general change in the mass. It generally means something which corrupts or depraves that with which it is mixed. Beware of the leaven of the Pharisees and of the Sadducees. Matthew 16:6.
To excite fermentation in; to raise and make light, as dough or paste. A little leaven leaveneth the whole lump. 7 Corinthians 5:6.
leavened: lev’ened. Taised and made light by fermentation.
leavening: lev’ening. that which leavens or makes light.
leaver: One who leaves or relinquishes; one who forsakes.
leaving: Quitting; withdrawing from; relinquishing; suffering to remain; ceasing; desisting from.
leavings: Things left; remnant; relics. The leavings of Pharsalia.
leavy: Full of leaves; covered with leaves.
lecher: A man given to lewdness; one addicted, in an exorbitant degree, to the indulgence of the animal appetite, and an illicit commerce with females.
to practice lewdness; to indulge lust.
lecherous: Addicted to lewdness; prone to indulge lust; lustful; lewd.
lecherousness: Lust, or strong propensity to indulge the sexual appetite.
lechery: Lewdness; free indulgence of lust; practice of indulging the animal appetite.
lection: A difference or variety in copies of a manuscript or book.
A lesson or portion of Scripture read in divine service.
lectionary: The Romish servicebook, containing portions of Scripture.
lecture: A discourse read or pronounced on any subject; usually, a formal or methodical discourse, intended for instruction; as a lecture on morals, philosophy, rhetoric, or theology.
A reading; the act or practice of reading; as in the lecture of Holy Scripture.
A magisterial reprimand; a formal reproof.
A recitation; rehearsal of a lesson.
To read or deliver a formal discourse.
lecturer: One who reads or pronounces lectures; a professor or an instructor who delivers formal discourses for the instruction of others.
A preacher in a church, hired by the parish to assist the rector, vicar or curate.
lecturing: Reading or delivering a discourse; reproving.
ledge: A stratum, layer or row. The lowest ledge or row should be merely of stone.
A ridge; a prominent row; as a ledge of rocks.
A prominent part; a regular part rising or projecting beyond the rest.
A small piece of timber placed athwart ships, under the deck between the beams.
ledger: The principal book of accounts among merchants; the book into which the accounts of the journal are carried in a summary form.
lee: Literally, a calm or sheltered place, a place defended from the wind; hence, that part of the hemisphers towards which the wind blows, as opposed to that from which it proceeds. Under the lee, denotes properly, in the part defended from the wind. Under the lee of the land, is properly, near the shore which breaks the force of the wind. Under the lee of a ship, on the side opposite to that on which the wind blows.
lee-board: A frame of plant affixed to the side of a flat-bottomed vessel, to prevent it from falling to leeward when close-hauled.
lee-gage: A greater distance from the point whence the wind blows, than another vessel has.
lee-lurch: A sudden and violent roll of a ship to leeward in a high sea.
lee-shore: The shore under the lee of a ship, or that towards which the wind blows.
lee-side: The side of a ship or boat farthest from the point whence the wind blows; opposed to the weather-side.
lee-tide: A tide running in the same direction that the wind blows. A tide under the lee, is a stream in an opposite direction to the wind.
leeward: Pertaining to the part towards which the wind blows; as a leeward ship.
Towards the lee, or that part towards which the wind blows; opposed to windward; as fall to leeward.
leeway: The lateral movement of a ship to the leeward of her course, or the angle which the line of her way makes with her keel, when she is close-hauled.
leech: A physician; a professor of the art of healing.
A blood-sucker; an animal of the genus Hirudo, a species of aquatic worm, which is used in the medical art for topical bleeding. One large species of this animal is called horse-leech.
In seamen’s language, the border or edge of a sail, which is sloping or perpendicular; as the fore-leech, the after-leech.
leech-line: Leech-lines are ropes fastened to the middle of the leeches of the main- sail and fore-sail, serving to truss them up to the yards.
leech-rope: That part of the bolt-rope to which the skirt or border of a sail is sewed.
leek: A plant of the genus Allium, with a bulbous root. Numbers 11:5.
leelite: A mineral, so called from Dr. Lee, of St. John’s College, Cambridge. It is described as a siliceous stone, and by some mineralogists considered to be a hydrate of silica.
leer: To look obliquely; to turn the eye and cast a look from a corner, either in contempt, defiance or frowning, or for a sly look.
To look with a forced countenance.
leering: Looking obliquely; casting a look askance.
leeringly: With an arch oblique look or smile.
lees: The grosser parts of any liquor which have settled on the bottom of a vessel; dregs; sediment; as the lees of wine.
leet: In Great Britain, a court. The court-leet or view of frankpledge, is a court of record held once a year and not oftener, within a particular hundred, lordship or manor, before the steward of the leet. Its original intent was to view the frankpledges or freemen within the liberty, to preserve the peace, and punish certain minute offenses. All freeholders within the precinct are obliged to attend this court. The court-leet is for the most part superseded by the county court.
leet-ale: A feast or merry making in the time of leet.
left: Denoting the part opposed to the right of the body; as the left hand, arm or side. Hence, the noun being omitted, we say, on the left, that is, on the left side or wing, as of an army.
The left bank of a river, is that which is on the left hand of a person whose face is towards the mouth of the river.
left-handed: Having the left hand or arm more strong and dextrous than the right; using the left hand and arm with more dexterity than the right.
left-handedness: Habitual use of the left hand, or rather the ability to use the left hand with more ease and strength than the right.
leg: The limb of an animal, used in supporting the body and in walking and running; properly, that part of the limb from the knee to the foot, but in a more general sense, the whole limb, including the thigh, the leg and the foot.

The long or slender support of any thing; as the leg of a table. To make a leg, to bow; a phrase introduced probably by the practice of drawing the right leg backward. To stand on one’s own legs, to support one’s self; to trust to one’s own strength or efforts without aid.
