luxuriant: Exuberant in growth; abundant; as a luxuriant growth of grass.
Exuberant in plenty; superfluous in abundance. Prune the luxuriant, the uncouth refine.
A luxuriant flower multiplies the covers of the fructification so as
luxuriate: To grow exuberantly, or to grow to superfluous abundance.
luxuriation: The process of growing exuberantly, or beyond the natural growth.
luxurious: Voluptuous; indulging freely or excessively in the pleasures of the table, the gratification of appetite, or in rich and expensive dress and equipage; as a luxurious life; luxurious cities.
Administering to luxury; contributing to free or extravagant indulgence in diet, dress and equipage; as luxurious wealth.
Furnished with luxuries; as a luxurious table.
Softening by pleasure, or free indulgence in luxury; as luxurious ease.
luxuriously: In abundance of rich diet, dress or equipage; deliciously; voluptuously. Noting a kind of soft slow music anciently in vogue.
luxury: A free or extravagant indulgence in the pleasures of the table, as in rich and expensive diet, or delicious food and liquors; voluptuousness in the gratification of appetite; or the free indulgence in costly dress and equipage. Riches expose a man to pride and luxury.
That which gratifies a nice and fastidious appetite; a dainty; any delicious food or drink. The canvas-back duck is a luxury for an epicure.
Any thing delightful to the senses. He cut the side of a rock for a garden, and by laying on it earth, furnished a king of luxury for a hermit.
ly: a termination of adjectives, is a contraction of Sax. lie, G. lich, D. lyk, Dan. lige, Sw. lik, Eng. like; as in lovely, manly, that is, love-like, man-like. As the termination of names, ly signifies field or plain, Sax. leag, Eng. lay, lea or ley, L. locus.
lyam: A leash for holding a hound.
lycostom: A Baltic fish resembling a herring.
lye: Water impregnated with alkaline salt imbibed from the ashes of wood.
lying: of lie. Telling falsehood. Lying in, being in childbirth. 1.n. The act of bearing a child.
lymnite: A kind of freshwater snail, found fossil.
lymph: Water, or a colorless fluid in animal bodies, separated from the blood and contained in certain vessels called lymphatics.
lymphatic: Pertaining to lymph. M is the thirteenth letter of the English Alphabet, and a labial articulation, formed by a compression of the lips. It is called a semi-vowel, as the articulation or compression of the lips is accompanied with a humming sound through the nose, which constitutes a difference between this letter and b. Its sound is uniform; as in man, timeM is a numeral letter, and among the ancients stood for a thousand; a use which is retained by the moderns. With a dash or stroke over it, it stands for a thousand times a thousand, or a million. M.A. or A.M. stands for artium magister, master of arts; M.D. for medicinoe doctor, doctor of medicine; A.M. for anno mundi, the year of the world; MS, for manuscript; MSS, for manuscripts.
A vessel of animal bodies which contains or conveys lymph. The lymphatics seem to perform the whole business of absorption.
lantern-ely: An insect of the genus Fulgora.
letters: The verbal expression; the literal meaning. LETTER. We must observe the letter of the law, without doing violence to the reason of the law, and the intentions of the lawgiver.
lifes: Eternal happiness in heaven. Romans 5:21. LIFE.
The author and giver of supreme felicity. LIFE. I am the way, the truth, and the life. John 14:6. LIFE.
A quickening, animating and strengthening principle, in a moral sense. John 6:63. LIFE.
The state of being in force, or the term for which an instrument has legal operation; as the life of an execution. LIFE.
lipt: To lift up the head, to raise from a low condition; to exalt. Genesis 40:13. LIFT.
To lift up the hand, to swear, or to confirm by oath. Genesis 14:22. LIFT.
To raise the hands in prayer. Psalm 28:2. LIFT.
To rise in opposition to; to rebel; to assault. 2 Samuel 18:28. LIFT.
lipograim: A writing in which a single letter is wholly omitted.
lympheduct: A vessel of animal bodies which conveys the lymph.
lymphography: A description of the lymphatic vessels, their origin and uses.
lynx: A quadruped of the genus Felis, resembling the common cat, but his ears are longer and his tail shorter. His hair is streaked with yellow, white and black colors. His air is sprightly; he howls like the wolf, and walks and leaps like a cat. This animal is celebrated for the sharpness of his sight.
lyrate: In botany, divided transversely into several jags, the lower ones smaller and more remote from each other than the upper ones; as a lyrate leaf.
lyrated: In botany, divided transversely into several jags, the lower ones smaller and more remote from each other than the upper ones; as a lyrate leaf.
lyre: A stringed instrument of music, a kind of harp much used by the ancients.
lyric: Pertaining to a lyre or harp. Lyric poetry is such as is sung to the harp or lyre. This was much cultivated by the ancients, among whom Anacreon, Alcoeus, Stesichorus, Sappho and Horace are distinguished as lyric poets.
lyrical: Pertaining to a lyre or harp. Lyric poetry is such as is sung to the harp or lyre. This was much cultivated by the ancients, among whom Anacreon, Alcoeus, Stesichorus, Sappho and Horace are distinguished as lyric poets.
lyrist: A musician who plays on the harp or lyre.
lys: A chinese measure of length, equal to 533 yards.
lyterian: In medical science, terminating a disease; indicating the solution of a disease.
lythrode: A mineral found in Norway; its color, an aurora- red, passing into brownish red or brown. It appears to be allied to elaolite, or fettstein. Lythrode is probably a variety of fettstein. L YTHRODE. M

M is the thirteenth letter of the English Alphabet, and a labial articulation, formed by a compression of the lips. It is called a semi-vowel, as the articulation or compression of the lips is accompanied with a humming sound through the nose, which constitutes a difference between this letter and b. Its sound is uniform; as in man, timeM is a numeral letter, and among the ancients stood for a thousand; a use which is retained by the moderns. With a dash or stroke over it, it stands for a thousand times a thousand, or a million. As an abbreviation, M stands for Marcus, Martius, Manlius or Mutius. lott: In architecture, a certain measure or size taken at pleasure for regulating the proportion of columns, and the symmetry or disposition of the whole building. The usual module of a column is its semidiameter at the base. This is divided into parts or minutes. MODULE.

Hence mother of pearl, the matrix of pearl.
